chapter_name: Organic Chemistry - Some Basic Principles and Techniques

concepts.name: Tetravalence of Carbon and Shapes of Organic Molecules
concepts.explanation: Carbon is tetravalent (forms four covalent bonds) and can undergo sp³, sp², or sp hybridisation depending on the number of sigma bonds, resulting in tetrahedral, trigonal planar, or linear geometries respectively.

concepts.name: Characteristic Features of π Bonds
concepts.explanation: π bonds are formed by sideways overlap of p orbitals. They restrict rotation around the double bond, have electron density above and below the plane, and are more reactive than σ bonds, making them centres for electrophilic attack.

concepts.name: Complete Structural Formula (Dash Formula)
concepts.explanation: In a complete structural formula, every covalent bond is represented by a dash (-). Single bonds are shown by a single dash, double bonds by two dashes, and triple bonds by three dashes.

concepts.name: Condensed Structural Formula
concepts.explanation: In a condensed formula, dashes representing bonds are omitted. Identical groups attached to an atom are indicated by subscripts. Example: CH₃CH₂CH₃ for propane; CH₃(CH₂)₆CH₃ for octane.

concepts.name: Bond-Line Structural Formula
concepts.explanation: In bond-line formulas, only lines representing carbon-carbon bonds are shown in a zig-zag fashion. Carbon and hydrogen atoms are not shown explicitly. Terminals represent CH₃ groups, and junctions represent carbon atoms.

concepts.name: Three-Dimensional Representation (Wedge-Dash Formula)
concepts.explanation: A solid wedge represents a bond projecting out of the plane of paper towards the observer; a dashed wedge represents a bond projecting away from the observer; a normal line represents a bond lying in the plane of paper.

concepts.name: Classification of Organic Compounds – Acyclic (Aliphatic)
concepts.explanation: Acyclic or open-chain compounds have carbon atoms arranged in straight or branched chains. They are called aliphatic compounds. Examples: butane (CH₃CH₂CH₂CH₃), isobutane.

concepts.name: Classification of Organic Compounds – Cyclic Compounds
concepts.explanation: Cyclic compounds have carbon atoms arranged in rings. They are classified as alicyclic (aliphatic cyclic, e.g., cyclohexane, cyclopropane) and aromatic (containing benzene ring or related structures).

concepts.name: Functional Groups
concepts.explanation: A functional group is an atom or group of atoms attached to the carbon chain that determines the characteristic chemical properties of the compound. Examples: -OH (alcohol), -CHO (aldehyde), -COOH (carboxylic acid).

concepts.name: Homologous Series
concepts.explanation: A homologous series is a group of organic compounds with the same functional group, similar chemical properties, and a general molecular formula. Successive members differ by -CH₂- unit. Example: alkanes (CₙH₂ₙ₊₂).

concepts.name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Alkanes – Unbranched Chains
concepts.explanation: Straight-chain alkanes are named with the prefix indicating the number of carbon atoms (meth-, eth-, prop-, but-, pent-, etc.) followed by the suffix '-ane'. Examples: CH₄ (methane), C₅H₁₂ (pentane).

concepts.name: Alkyl Groups
concepts.explanation: An alkyl group is derived from an alkane by removing one hydrogen atom. Its general formula is CₙH₂ₙ₊₁. Examples: -CH₃ (methyl), -CH₂CH₃ (ethyl), -CH₂CH₂CH₃ (propyl). Common abbreviations: Me, Et, Pr, Bu.

concepts.name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Branched Chain Alkanes
concepts.explanation: Rules: (1) Identify the longest carbon chain (parent). (2) Number to give the lowest numbers to substituents. (3) Name substituents as alkyl groups with their positions. (4) List substituents alphabetically. Prefixes di, tri, etc. are used for identical groups.

concepts.name: Nomenclature of Compounds with Functional Groups
concepts.explanation: The functional group determines the suffix (e.g., -ol for alcohol, -al for aldehyde, -one for ketone). The longest chain containing the functional group is numbered to give the functional group the lowest possible number.

concepts.name: Nomenclature of Polyfunctional Compounds
concepts.explanation: When multiple functional groups are present, the principal functional group (highest priority) determines the suffix; other functional groups are named as prefixes. Example: HOCH₂CH₂COCH₃ is 7-hydroxyheptan-2-one (ketone is principal).

concepts.name: Nomenclature of Substituted Benzene Compounds
concepts.explanation: Substituents are named as prefixes to benzene (e.g., chlorobenzene, nitrobenzene). For disubstituted benzene, ortho (1,2), meta (1,3), and para (1,4) prefixes are used. For tri-substituted, use lowest locant rule.

concepts.name: Structural Isomerism – Chain Isomerism
concepts.explanation: Chain isomerism occurs when compounds have the same molecular formula but different carbon skeletons. Example: C₅H₁₂ has three chain isomers: n-pentane, isopentane, and neopentane.

concepts.name: Structural Isomerism – Position Isomerism
concepts.explanation: Position isomerism occurs when the same functional group or substituent is attached at different positions on the same carbon skeleton. Example: C₃H₈O has propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol.

concepts.name: Structural Isomerism – Functional Group Isomerism
concepts.explanation: Functional group isomerism occurs when compounds have the same molecular formula but different functional groups. Example: C₃H₆O can be propanal (aldehyde) or propanone (ketone).

concepts.name: Structural Isomerism – Metamerism
concepts.explanation: Metamerism arises when different alkyl groups are attached to the same functional group. Example: C₄H₁₀O has methoxypropane (CH₃OC₃H₇) and ethoxyethane (C₂H₅OC₂H₅).

concepts.name: Stereoisomerism – Introduction
concepts.explanation: Stereoisomers have the same constitution and sequence of covalent bonds but differ in the spatial arrangement of atoms or groups. They are classified as geometrical (cis-trans) and optical isomers.

concepts.name: Heterolytic Bond Fission – Carbocation and Carbanion
concepts.explanation: In heterolytic fission, the shared pair of electrons goes to one fragment. If carbon loses the electron pair, a carbocation (R₃C⁺) is formed (sp² hybridised, trigonal planar). If carbon gains the electron pair, a carbanion (R₃C⁻) is formed (sp³ hybridised, tetrahedral).
concepts.formula_reaction: R-X → R⁺ + X⁻ (carbocation); R-X → R⁻ + X⁺ (carbanion)

concepts.name: Homolytic Bond Fission – Free Radicals
concepts.explanation: In homolytic fission, the bond breaks symmetrically, with one electron going to each atom. This produces free radicals (neutral species with an unpaired electron). Stability order: tertiary > secondary > primary > methyl.

concepts.name: Nucleophiles and Electrophiles
concepts.explanation: A nucleophile (Nu:) is an electron-rich species that donates an electron pair to form a bond (e.g., HO⁻, CN⁻, NH₃). An electrophile (E⁺) is an electron-deficient species that accepts an electron pair (e.g., carbocations, BF₃).

concepts.name: Curved-Arrow Notation in Organic Reactions
concepts.explanation: Curved arrows show the movement of electron pairs. A full-headed arrow (→) indicates the shift of an electron pair. A fish-hook arrow (→) indicates the movement of a single electron (free radical reactions).

concepts.name: Inductive Effect (+I and -I)
concepts.explanation: The inductive effect is the permanent polarisation of a σ bond due to the electronegativity difference between atoms. Electron-donating groups (+I, e.g., alkyl groups) push electrons; electron-withdrawing groups (-I, e.g., -NO₂, -CN, halogens) pull electrons.

concepts.name: Resonance Effect (+R and -R)
concepts.explanation: The resonance effect is the polarity produced in a molecule by the interaction of π bonds or between a π bond and a lone pair on an adjacent atom. +R groups donate electrons (e.g., -OH, -NH₂, halogens); -R groups withdraw electrons (e.g., -NO₂, -CN, -CHO).

concepts.name: Electromeric Effect (+E and -E)
concepts.explanation: The electromeric effect is a temporary effect where π electrons are completely transferred to one atom of a multiple bond in the presence of an attacking reagent. It operates only during the reaction and is annulled when the reagent is removed.

concepts.name: Hyperconjugation (No-Bond Resonance)
concepts.explanation: Hyperconjugation involves the delocalisation of σ electrons from a C-H bond (adjacent to an unsaturated system or a carbocation) into an empty p orbital or π system. It stabilises carbocations and alkenes. More alkyl groups → more C-H bonds → greater stabilisation.

concepts.name: Types of Organic Reactions – Substitution
concepts.explanation: In substitution reactions, an atom or group in a molecule is replaced by another atom or group. Examples: halogenation of alkanes (free radical substitution), nucleophilic substitution (e.g., R-X + OH⁻ → R-OH).

concepts.name: Types of Organic Reactions – Addition
concepts.explanation: In addition reactions, two or more molecules combine to form a single product. Common in unsaturated compounds (alkenes, alkynes). Examples: hydrogenation, halogenation, addition of HBr to alkenes.

concepts.name: Types of Organic Reactions – Elimination
concepts.explanation: In elimination reactions, atoms or groups are removed from adjacent atoms to form a multiple bond. Examples: dehydration of alcohols (elimination of H₂O), dehydrohalogenation of alkyl halides (elimination of HX).

concepts.name: Types of Organic Reactions – Rearrangement
concepts.explanation: In rearrangement reactions, the carbon skeleton of a molecule is reorganized to form a structural isomer of the original compound. Example: migration of groups to form a more stable carbocation.

concepts.name: Purification – Sublimation
concepts.explanation: Sublimation is used to purify solids that directly change from solid to vapour state without melting. The vapour condenses back to pure solid. Examples: iodine, camphor, ammonium chloride.

concepts.name: Purification – Crystallisation
concepts.explanation: Crystallisation is based on the difference in solubility of the compound and impurities in a solvent. The compound dissolves at high temperature and crystallises on cooling. Repeated crystallisation may be needed for high purity.

concepts.name: Purification – Simple Distillation
concepts.explanation: Simple distillation separates volatile liquids from non-volatile impurities or liquids with a large boiling point difference (>50 K). The liquid is boiled, vapours are condensed, and the distillate is collected.

concepts.name: Purification – Fractional Distillation
concepts.explanation: Fractional distillation separates liquids with close boiling points using a fractionating column. The column provides multiple vaporisation-condensation cycles. Used in petroleum refining (separating crude oil fractions).

concepts.name: Purification – Distillation Under Reduced Pressure
concepts.explanation: Used for liquids with high boiling points or those that decompose at their boiling points. By reducing the pressure, the liquid boils at a lower temperature. Example: glycerol purification.
concepts.formula_reaction: Boiling point decreases as pressure decreases

concepts.name: Purification – Steam Distillation
concepts.explanation: Used for steam-volatile, water-immiscible compounds. Steam is passed through the mixture; the compound vaporises below its normal boiling point because the total vapour pressure equals atmospheric pressure (p = p_compound + p_water).

concepts.name: Purification – Differential Extraction
concepts.explanation: An organic compound is extracted from an aqueous solution by shaking with an immiscible organic solvent in which the compound is more soluble. The layers are separated using a separating funnel.

concepts.name: Purification – Chromatography
concepts.explanation: Chromatography separates components of a mixture based on differential adsorption on a stationary phase. Types include: column chromatography, thin-layer chromatography (TLC), and paper chromatography.

concepts.name: Lassaigne's Test – Detection of N, S, Halogens, P
concepts.explanation: Organic compounds are fused with sodium metal to convert covalent elements into ionic form: NaCN (N), Na₂S (S), NaX (halogens). The sodium fusion extract is tested: N gives Prussian blue; S gives black PbS or violet with sodium nitroprusside; halogens give AgX precipitates.
concepts.formula_reaction: Na + C + N → NaCN; 2Na + S → Na₂S; Na + X → NaX

concepts.name: Detection of Carbon and Hydrogen
concepts.explanation: The compound is heated with CuO. Carbon is oxidised to CO₂ (turns lime water milky). Hydrogen is oxidised to H₂O (turns anhydrous CuSO₄ blue).
concepts.formula_reaction: C + 2CuO → 2Cu + CO₂; H₂ + CuO → Cu + H₂O

concepts.name: Quantitative Analysis – Estimation of C and H
concepts.explanation: A known mass of compound is burnt; CO₂ is absorbed in KOH solution (U-tube) and H₂O in anhydrous CaCl₂. The increase in mass gives the amounts of CO₂ and H₂O. %C = (12/44) × (mass CO₂/mass compound) × 100; %H = (2/18) × (mass H₂O/mass compound) × 100.

concepts.name: Quantitative Analysis – Dumas Method for Nitrogen
concepts.explanation: The compound is heated with CuO in a CO₂ atmosphere; nitrogen is liberated. The gas is collected over KOH solution (absorbs CO₂). The volume of N₂ is measured and percentage of nitrogen is calculated.
concepts.formula_reaction: %N = (28 × V × 100) / (22400 × m) (V = volume of N₂ at STP)

concepts.name: Quantitative Analysis – Kjeldahl's Method for Nitrogen
concepts.explanation: The compound is heated with conc. H₂SO₄ to convert N to (NH₄)₂SO₄. Then NaOH is added to liberate NH₃, which is absorbed in excess standard H₂SO₄. The unreacted acid is back-titrated. Not applicable for nitro, azo, and ring nitrogen compounds.
concepts.formula_reaction: %N = (1.4 × M × 2(V - V₁/2)) / m (where M is molarity of acid)

concepts.name: Quantitative Analysis – Carius Method for Halogens
concepts.explanation: The compound is heated with fuming HNO₃ and AgNO₃ in a Carius tube. The halogen forms AgX precipitate, which is filtered, dried, and weighed. % halogen is calculated from AgX mass.
concepts.formula_reaction: %X = (Atomic mass of X × mass AgX × 100) / (Molar mass of AgX × mass compound)

concepts.name: Quantitative Analysis – Estimation of Sulphur
concepts.explanation: The compound is heated with fuming HNO₃ or Na₂O₂ in a Carius tube. Sulphur is oxidised to H₂SO₄, which is precipitated as BaSO₄. The precipitate is filtered, dried, and weighed.

concepts.name: Quantitative Analysis – Estimation of Phosphorus
concepts.explanation: The compound is heated with fuming HNO₃. Phosphorus is oxidised to H₃PO₄, which is precipitated as ammonium phosphomolybdate [(NH₄)₃PO₄·12MoO₃] or Mg₂P₂O₇ (by ignition of MgNH₄PO₄).

concepts.name: Quantitative Analysis – Estimation of Oxygen
concepts.explanation: Oxygen is usually determined by difference: %O = 100 - (%C + %H + %N + %S + %X + %P). Direct method: oxygen is converted to CO over red-hot coke, then oxidised to CO₂ by I₂O₅. Each mole of O₂ produces 2 moles of CO₂.
